meeting_purpose_blurb: Council examines particular topics in depth. No official decisions are made and no public comment is taken.
lead_headline: Arvada Council Examines Wildfire Prep and Utility Rate Roadmap

type_specific.meeting_summary: Arvada City Council held two workshops on May 12, 2026. The first examined wildfire mitigation and response strategies, with presentations from city emergency management and Arvada Fire covering preparedness levels, the Community Wildfire Protection Plan, the new wildfire resiliency code, public safety power shut-offs, prescribed burning, and the Jefferson County hazard mitigation plan update. The second workshop kicked off the annual utility rate-setting process, with staff presenting foundational information on water, wastewater, and stormwater finances, affordability programs, infrastructure prioritization, and a proposed community advisory group.
type_specific.lead_headline: Arvada Council Examines Wildfire Prep and Utility Rate Roadmap
type_specific.meeting_purpose_blurb: Council examines particular topics in depth. No official decisions are made and no public comment is taken.

workshop_topics.title: Wildfire Mitigation and Response Strategies
workshop_topics.tagline: City and Arvada Fire present layered approach to wildfire readiness, evacuation planning, and community education
workshop_topics.lede: City emergency management staff and Arvada Fire representatives jointly briefed council on the city's multi-layered wildfire preparedness approach. The presentation covered the recently completed Community Wildfire Protection Plan (CWPP), the newly adopted wildfire resiliency code, public safety power shut-offs (PSPS) managed by Xcel Energy, prescribed burning, evacuation zone planning, and the ongoing Jefferson County hazard mitigation plan update. Staff noted that some policy gaps remain, particularly around integrating evacuation analysis into development review, and that Lookout Alert notification system enrollment still needs to grow.

options.label: Current preparedness and mitigation framework
options.summary: Continue and expand the existing multi-agency approach: tiered fire department response levels, EOC pre-activation on high-risk days, neighborhood education, home assessments, mechanical fuel reduction projects, Lookout Alert enrollment drives, and CWPP-guided prioritization of mitigation funding.

options.label: Add formal evacuation policy tools for development review
options.summary: Create ordinance or policy mechanisms that formally require evacuation analysis as part of development proposals, similar to models used in Colorado Springs and Boulder County, moving beyond the current informal staff consultation approach.

options.label: Prescribed burning program
options.summary: Use controlled burns on identified open space areas to proactively reduce fuel loads, subject to regulatory approvals from CDPHE, Jefferson County, and the Colorado Division of Fire Prevention and Control.

options.label: Expanded PSPS community mitigation measures
options.summary: Coordinate with Jefferson County to pre-position generators at critical intersections, set up charging stations powered by solar generators, staff warming centers and shelters, and develop scalable response formulas based on the size of each shut-off event.

member_positions.name: Bob Fifer
member_positions.off_menu_summary: Engage Jefferson County mitigation crews for unincorporated fire-risk areas

quote.speaker: Bob Fifer
quote.text: I was talking to Brian over there keing Keing about those mitigation crews. So if they're not fighting fires, can they get that whole crew just rolling? Um, obviously they got to hire 45 people or whatever, but um, I just curious how much we're engaged with them to address that since some of it's out of the city.
quote.context_excerpt: Yeah, I was talking to Brian over there keing Keing about those mitigation crews. So if they're not fighting fires, can they get that whole crew just rolling? Um, obviously they got to hire 45 people or whatever, but um, I just curious how much we're engaged with them to address that since some of it's out of the city. Um, as just a side note, just so you know, and I think you know this, you know, Highway 72 to Highway 119 is the highest burn probability in the state.

member_positions.name: Randy Moorman
member_positions.off_menu_summary: Publish pre-event PSPS resource locations for residents to plan ahead

quote.speaker: Randy Moorman
quote.text: I'm also thinking about what we can do in the immediate term to help our residents. Um, and this, I guess, maybe is a sort of a request for support. Are there information that we can be sharing out now about, you know, what to have in your go bag? I know we have that, but where will charging stations be set up? Where can the public just know in advance before uh an event happens?
quote.context_excerpt: However, I'm also thinking about what we can do in the immediate term to help our residents. Um, and this, I guess, maybe is a sort of a request for support. Are there information that we can be sharing out now about, you know, what to have in your go bag? I know we have that, but where will charging stations be set up? Where can the public just know in advance before uh an event happens? Okay, I'm going to have to work from home today, but I'm going to grab my laptop and I'm going to go here.

member_positions.name: Lauren Simpson
member_positions.off_menu_summary: Renewables and microgrids for PSPS resilience tied to climate action plan

quote.speaker: Randy Moorman
quote.text: I'm going to sound like a broken record council but I think you know having a more sustainable local source of energy uh to provide our residents in those times of the shut off whether it's a micro grid system, you know, solar gardens that we can tap into in battery system. There's many things we can be looking at to help have a more robust, resilient community when we do have those power shut offs.
quote.context_excerpt: and so I hope you're in coordination with that because you know especially comes to mind the public safety power shut offs and you know I'm I'm going to sound like a broken record council but I think you know having a more sustainable local source of energy uh to provide our residents in those times of the shut off whether it's a micro grid system, you know, solar gardens that we can tap into in battery system. There's many things we can be looking at to help have a more robust, resilient community when we do have those power shut offs.

quote.speaker: Rebecka Lovisone
quote.text: I second goats in particular because they are they can eat anything. They they're even better than cows in that respect.
quote.context_excerpt: I I'm pretty sure we had an al it was either a llama or an alpaca running down Highway 93 the other day. It was very funny. Um I second goats in particular because they are they can eat anything. They they're even better than cows in that respect. Um I only had really one question. Um Mayor Proim Mormon was sort of elucidating some of the long-term things we can do in terms of the upcoming uh PSPS PS uh events.

workshop_topics.feeds_into: Fall 2026 business meeting (Jefferson County Hazard Mitigation Plan adoption); future business meeting for evacuation policy ordinance if staff develops proposal

workshop_topics.title: 2027 Water, Wastewater, and Stormwater Rates and Fees
workshop_topics.tagline: Staff launches annual utility rate workshop series with financial overview, affordability update, and new community advisory group proposal
workshop_topics.lede: Utilities staff opened the first of three to four planned workshops leading up to an October rate adoption. Staff provided foundational information on how the city's enterprise utility funds work, reviewed the current bill structure for water, sewer, stormwater, and waste services, and described the multi-year 'roadmap' investment strategy that has included $205 million in bond issuances since 2022. Staff also presented updates to customer affordability programs and proposed forming a new ad hoc community advisory group to engage residents in utility decision-making through approximately 2030.

options.label: Community advisory group (ad hoc, through ~2030)
options.summary: Stand up a new short-term, ad hoc advisory group of community members to provide input on infrastructure prioritization and rate-setting trade-offs during the current historic reinvestment period, distinct from existing boards and commissions.

options.label: Continue roadmap rate adjustment path
options.summary: Continue steady annual rate and fee adjustments consistent with the 2022 'roadmap' scenario, funding both repair-and-replacement of aging infrastructure and growth-related capacity, supported by bond debt and grants.

options.label: Drought surcharge tiers and conservation pricing
options.summary: Explore adding drought surcharges at higher usage tiers and revising the tiered rate structure, potentially in ways that account for household size or specific productive water uses such as vegetable gardens or tree irrigation.

quote.speaker: Randy Moorman
quote.text: I first just want to commend you on the um the community advisory group. I think that's a fantastic idea and I'm I'm excited to see that get going.
quote.context_excerpt: uh thank you. I I first just want to commend you on the um the community advisory group. I think that's a fantastic idea and I'm I'm excited to see that get going. Um happy to help get the word out. So, is there on our website now a place to apply? Is that correct?

member_positions.name: Rebecka Lovisone
member_positions.off_menu_summary: Assess existing boards before adding new advisory committee

quote.speaker: Rebecka Lovisone
quote.text: I would just also like to encourage us to pause whenever possible before creating additional boards and commissions or committees until we create clearer structural alignment for the groups we already have. Uh so just a thought moving forward before adding something new. I think we should first ask is there an existing combination of boards and commissions that could advise the work who already exists.
quote.context_excerpt: that said, I would just also like to encourage us to pause whenever possible before creating additional boards and commissions or committees until we create clearer structural alignment for the groups we already have. Uh so just a thought moving forward before adding something new. I think we should first ask is there an existing combination of boards and commissions that could advise the work who already exists um and just figuring out how maybe if possible we can engage um some folks already engaged in the community.

member_positions.name: Bob Fifer
member_positions.off_menu_summary: Revisit tier formulas to account for household size during drought pricing

quote.speaker: Bob Fifer
quote.text: the size of family. Um, I've always had this concern and I know I'm not going to bring it up. We don't need to debate it because it's hard for the city to determine how many people are in the house. But the issue is is like a house of two can meet the first tier or less easily, but once you have like I had three boys at a time. I was never in the first tier. I was pushed into the second tier. And then I got penalized and paid more money because I had more kids.
quote.context_excerpt: But the second thing uh I want to bring up around the water rates or not necessarily water rates but drought when we get into the drought situations is the size of family. Um, I've always had this concern and I know I'm not going to bring it up. We don't need to debate it because it's hard for the city to determine how many people are in the house. But the issue is is like a house of two can meet the first tier or less easily, but once you have like I had three boys at a time.

member_positions.name: Michael Griffith
member_positions.off_menu_summary: Align water rates with tree canopy and urban agriculture goals via forestry part

quote.speaker: Michael Griffith
quote.text: my concern is that when we talk about water conservation um and water usage in rural versus urban context um obviously it's more efficient in urban context. We're using less water because per person, per square foot, all that, right? But there's a co- benefit to basically vegetation, greenery, tree canopy, all these things that happen in more rural contexts that become very difficult to manage um with water rates that are flat.
quote.context_excerpt: Um I wanted to talk about something that's just a general concern of mine and maybe um I don't really have a solution for it but it's maybe something that we could talk about as we move through for future workshops. Um my concern is that when we talk about water conservation um and water usage in rural versus urban context um obviously it's more efficient in urban context. We're using less water because per person, per square foot, all that, right? But there's a co- benefit to basically vegetation, greenery, tree canopy.

member_positions.name: Shawna Ambrose
member_positions.off_menu_summary: Reporting on payment compliance, liens, and water shut-off outcomes

quote.speaker: Shawna Ambrose
quote.text: I also recall at that time we switched from doing leans and no water shut offs to doing water shut offs that could lead to condemnations. And so I just would like a report um always to have a report um anytime you do a presentation on um the payment compliance because I also know that switching that brought so many more households into compliance to being current on their utilities which is a huge improvement.
quote.context_excerpt: Um uh I also recall at that time we switched from doing leans and no water shut offs to doing water shut offs that could lead to condemnations. And so I just would like a report um always to have a report um anytime you do a presentation on um the payment compliance because I also know that switching that brought so many more households into compliance to being current on their utilities which is a huge improvement. But I also am always concerned about how many condemnations have happened just due to the inability to pay for water.

member_positions.name: Lauren Simpson
member_positions.off_menu_summary: Add 'completed projects' green category to infrastructure prioritization spreads

quote.speaker: Lauren Simpson
quote.text: There's no green you guys. We always want to celebrate the greens, the things that we have delivered on to maybe it can be on a separate page, but just to show the public, you know, it's not all red and orange and yellow that, you know, we have had these bonds working for the last several years. Here are the things we've delivered and we can and it makes us feel good when we start to see the stackup of all the things that we've delivered for our residents.
quote.context_excerpt: I'm going to channel a thought council member Feifer somehow did not bring up. I I thought for sure uh let's go back to our colors. Red, yellow, red, orange, and yellow. There's no green you guys. We always want to celebrate the greens, the things that we have delivered on to maybe it can be on a separate page, but just to show the public, you know, it's not all red and orange and yellow that, you know, we have had these bonds working for the last several years.

workshop_topics.feeds_into: July 28, 2026 utility workshop (rate scenarios); August 25, 2026 follow-up workshop; October 2026 business meeting (first and second reading of rate adoption)

meeting_summary: Arvada City Council held two workshops on May 12, 2026. The first examined wildfire mitigation and response strategies, with presentations from city emergency management and Arvada Fire covering preparedness levels, the Community Wildfire Protection Plan, the new wildfire resiliency code, public safety power shut-offs, prescribed burning, and the Jefferson County hazard mitigation plan update. The second workshop kicked off the annual utility rate-setting process, with staff presenting foundational information on water, wastewater, and stormwater finances, affordability programs, infrastructure prioritization, and a proposed community advisory group.

alerts.title: Free Drop-Off Site Open at Long Lake
alerts.body: The City of Arvada is hosting a free tree limb drop-off starting Saturday, May 9 at Long Lake Regional Park.
